main_summary.summary: Dorothy "Dottie" Foreman Zellner, recorded December 8, 2015 in Baltimore by Emilye Crosby for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts her trajectory from a New York Jewish immigrant left-wing household into SNCC's Atlanta headquarters. Born January 14, 1938 to parents who escaped Lithuanian and Ukrainian Jewish antisemitism, she came of age in McCarthy-era New York; the Rosenberg execution and her family's Smith Act prosecution shaped her political formation. After reading about the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, she joined CORE's June 1960 Miami workshop, was arrested at a New Orleans Woolworth sit-in, then took a Southern Regional Council research position in Atlanta in June 1961 producing the 18-month SRC sit-in report. After SRC fired her following an FBI visit, James Forman hired her at SNCC in October 1961. She typed field affidavits sent to Robert F. Kennedy, edited the Student Voice with Julian Bond, was hospitalized after a police beating in the Danville campaign, worked Mississippi Freedom Summer's WATS line in 1964, ran SNCC's Boston Northeast Regional Office, and in May 1967 co-authored with Bob Zellner the rejected proposal to retain white staff voting rights, after which they joined the Southern Conference Educational Fund.
key_themes: Jewish left-wing political formation and McCarthyism
SNCC administrative infrastructure and James Forman's leadership
Interracial organizing and the white-staff question
Press strategy and the Student Voice
Volunteer vetting for Mississippi Freedom Summer
main_summary.historical_significance:
Zellner offers a rare interior view of SNCC's Atlanta nerve center from October 1961 through 1967, the period when James Forman built the hub-and-wheel administrative architecture (Friends of SNCC support groups, field-secretary affidavit pipeline to Robert F. Kennedy, motor pool, campus travelers) that sustained Mississippi and Southwest Georgia projects. Her account corrects the historiographic record on Forman, whom she describes as containing crises in Greenwood, Mississippi and Danville, Virginia rather than the volatile portrayals in some published accounts and in the film Selma.

The testimony is also a primary source on the 1963 SNCC civil liberties workshop convened by Anne Braden and Ella Baker, a previously unsung decision to refuse loyalty oaths and to take the First Amendment rather than the Fifth if subpoenaed by HUAC. Zellner's recollection of the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates staff meeting (where the proposal she drafted with Bob Zellner was rejected) and her insistence on telling press the truth (including accurate demonstration counts) document SNCC's internal craft of organizing and communication.

chapters.title: Jewish Immigrant Childhood in Wartime New York
chapters.summary: Zellner describes her January 14, 1938 birth in New York City to Lithuanian-British and Ukrainian-Jewish immigrant parents. Her dentist father and his nurse-wife operated out of their apartment; both were left-wing secular Jews. World War II, the Holocaust (she discovered The Black Book at age eight), and her father's solidarity with Spanish Civil War veterans were the defining childhood frames.

chapters.title: McCarthyism and Political Awakening
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts a Communist Party family arrested under the Smith Act in their apartment building in 1951, her high-school class's blocked invitation of J. Robert Oppenheimer as a 1954 graduation speaker, and the formative trip to picket the White House in 1953 during the Julius and Ethel Rosenberg execution stay. She joined the NAACP in high school and recalls meeting Paul Robeson on Second Avenue.

chapters.title: CORE Workshop and New Orleans Sit-In Arrest
chapters.summary: Reading the Greensboro sit-in story in February 1960 galvanized Zellner. Through a CORE contact she joined a June 1960 Miami workshop with Rudy Lombard, Hugh Murray, the Stephens sisters, and Angeline Butler. Arrested at a Miami restaurant sit-in, she then traveled to New Orleans where she canvassed a Woolworth's on Canal Street, met Father Joseph Fichter at Loyola University New Orleans, and was thrown out of Rabbi Julian Feibelman's office. Bernard Lafayette directed her to SNCC.

chapters.title: Southern Regional Council and FBI Firing
chapters.summary: Hired by Dr. James A. Moss in June 1961 as a research associate at the Southern Regional Council, Zellner conducted the newspaper-clipping research behind SRC's 18-month sit-in report (heavily edited by Leslie Dunbar). She lived on the white side of Atlanta with Jane Bond at 2222 Tilson Street. After an FBI visit produced a photograph of her, Dunbar fired her, citing redundancy, possibly linked to her participation in a Howard Zinn-organized Cuban Missile Crisis demonstration.

chapters.title: Joining SNCC Under James Forman
chapters.summary: Volunteering at SNCC nights from October 1961, Zellner met James Forman, who had arrived only a month earlier. After SRC fired her she joined SNCC's staff full-time. She typed field affidavits dictated by returning field secretaries, affidavits Forman sent to Robert F. Kennedy invoking the 1957 Civil Rights Act. She describes Forman as the architect of SNCC's wheel-and-hub administrative structure: Friends of SNCC support groups, campus travelers, motor pool.

chapters.title: Danville, Virginia and Greenwood, Mississippi
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the 1963 Greenwood, Mississippi police stop alongside Bob Dylan's folk-festival visit, and the June 1963 Danville, Virginia Dan River Mills campaign where she was beaten unconscious by a policeman's club, captured in a Danny Lyon photograph of Forman taking her affidavit. She describes Forman saving the group during a second Danville demonstration by distracting the police chief while signaling staff to retreat from imminent fire-hose-and-club violence.

chapters.title: James Forman's Character and Decline
chapters.summary: Zellner mounts an extended defense of Forman's reputation against historiographic portrayals of him as volatile. She describes his cultural sophistication, French fluency, ruthless press discipline, and the buying of twenty SNCC cars at $2,000 each. She traces his three psychiatric breakdowns starting in 1968, including Constance "Dinky" Romilly's role rescuing him and Elizabeth "Betita" Sutherland Martínez's role helping him write his book, through his stabilized but altered later years.

chapters.title: Student Voice and Press Strategy with Julian Bond
chapters.summary: From the winter of 1961-62, Zellner worked with Julian Bond on SNCC communications, laying out dummies of the Student Voice newspaper, taking it to a black printer associated with the Atlanta Inquirer. They taught field staff and summer volunteers to give the press truthful counts ("forty-two, not five hundred") and trained on telephone-interview discipline. She identifies the deliberate practice of racially identifying Black people in press releases (with white as default).

chapters.title: Boston Northeast Regional Office and Volunteer Vetting
chapters.summary: After marrying Bob Zellner in August 1963 and moving to Cambridge for his graduate study, Zellner ran SNCC's Northeast Regional Office in Boston, emphasizing she was a salaried staff member, not a Friends of SNCC volunteer. She and others rigorously interviewed Mississippi Summer Project applicants using COFO's questionnaire, screening for adventurers and divas. She admits to letting Abbie Hoffman through (then in suit and tie), a volunteer who later publicly attacked SNCC's treatment of white staff.

chapters.title: 1963 Civil Liberties Workshop and the SNCC No-Cooperation Decision
chapters.summary: Zellner identifies the spring 1963 SNCC civil-liberties workshop, convened jointly by Anne Braden and Ella Baker, as a historically underdocumented turning point. Surviving McCarthyism with Carl Braden's year-long contempt-of-Congress imprisonment as personal context, SNCC voted to refuse loyalty oaths and to take the First Amendment rather than the Fifth if subpoenaed. She argues an undercover agent must have transmitted the decision to the federal government, which then refrained from subpoenaing the sit-in heroes for international-image reasons.

chapters.title: The Peg Leg Bates Vote and Leaving SNCC
chapters.summary: Zellner recounts the May 1967 Peg Leg Bates central-committee meeting where she drafted a proposal, presented by Bob Zellner, to retain their voting rights as SNCC staff while organizing in the white community. The proposal was rejected. She argues SNCC's collapse was not primarily caused by white staff departure but by Atlantic City disillusionment and the country's deep racism. The Zellners then joined Anne Braden's Southern Conference Educational Fund (SCEF) in New Orleans for five years.

chapters.title: Ruby Doris Smith Robinson and Mississippi Freedom Summer Reflections
chapters.summary: Zellner remembers Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, SNCC's de facto second-in-command, as someone the men were afraid of, and who became gentle only during her terminal illness at New York's Beth Israel Hospital. Zellner attributes Ruby's collapse to the 30-day rock-breaking sentence at Parchman, dismissing the government-poisoning theory. She closes with reflections on Mississippi Freedom Summer's WATS-line work, the unrealized 1965 summer project, and Allard K. Lowenstein-coalition efforts to displace SNCC.

loc_video.caption: Dorothy Zellner oral history interview conducted by Emilye Crosby in Baltimore, Maryland, 2015 December 08
